B: Why are you dressed like that?
I: La mode kabyle algérienne est un pilier du patrimoine vestimentaire amazigh, caractérisée par la Taqendurt (robe), reconnue par ses couleurs vives (rouge, jaune, vert, bleu), ses broderies géométriques, ses galons (sifa) et ses tissus légers. Souvent accompagnées de bijoux en argent émaillé.
J:
Pourquoi es-tu habillé comme ça ? » Family Dinner fait référence à une tendance sur Twitter censée évoquer une famille élargie traditionnelle, surprise par l’apparence d’un parent qu’elle voit rarement. Le format consiste en deux images successives : le texte « why are you dressed like that » sert de légende à la première image, et « like what? » à la seconde. 
La première image montre une grande famille blanche assise autour d’une table de dîner, les assiettes pleines, et tous les membres fixant le spectateur. 
La seconde image montre la personne à qui la famille s’adresse, avec une apparence inhabituelle que, selon la légende, elle considère comme normale.

I:
La mode maximaliste est une esthétique qui prône le « plus c'est mieux », célébrant l'excès, l'expression audacieuse et les associations éclectiques ; elle est souvent considérée comme un contre-courant direct au minimalisme. Parmi ses éléments clés, on retrouve des couleurs vives, des motifs contrastés, des textures variées et des accessoires spectaculaires et surdimensionnés, qui permettent de créer un look très personnel, théâtral et artistique.
Le maximalisme apparaît souvent comme une réaction contre les styles restrictifs et minimalistes, ou lors de périodes de changement social, s'inspirant d'époques historiques telles que les années 1970 et 1980, mais egalemet plus loin jusqu'à remonter aux inspiration des Empires du monde ancien. Mode maximaliste ; mode années 1980s ; more is more. Superpositions et textures : associer différentes matières telles que les paillettes, le velours, la fausse fourrure et les broderies complexes pour créer de la profondeur. Motifs et couleurs audacieux : mélanger des imprimés animaliers, floraux et des formes géométriques dans une même tenue, souvent qualifiée de « déferlement de couleurs ». Accessoires phares : utiliser des bijoux surdimensionnés, des chapeaux spectaculaires et des sacs à main originaux pour accentuer le look. Chaos organisé : bien qu'éclectique, le maximalisme consiste en une sélection délibérée d'éléments plutôt qu'en un désordre, visant une esthétique « chaotiquement cohérente ».
J:
Tendance « Forget clean girl », alias esthétique Amazigh/Arab Mob Wife 
Le 7 octobre 2022, la TikToker @thesweetpaisana a publié une vidéo dans laquelle elle sort d'une voiture vêtue d'un long manteau de fourrure par-dessus une tenue plus neutre, avec la légende : « Hot girl summer is over, welcome to mob wife fall » (L'été des filles sexy est terminé, bienvenue à l'automne des femmes de gangsters). L’esthétique Mob Wife, qui évoque cette tendance, est censée évoquer la confiance en soi et la richesse ; elle s’inspire de la représentation des épouses de mafieux dans les films et les séries télévisées. Le look comporte généralement des imprimés animal, comme le léopard, des manteaux de fourrure (souvent vintage), des vêtements en cuir, de la fausse fourrure et des bottes noires. Le 15 Janvier, le réalisateur Francis Ford Coppola a publié un post sur Instagram avec la légende : « J’ai entendu dire que l’esthétique Mob Wife fait son grand retour… ». L’exemple de l'utilisation de la tendence avec la tournure "Forget Clean Girl" par @womenfromhistory et “Oublie le style Clean Girl, je veux avoir l’air de venir de l’Empire Achéménide”, autrement dit l'Empire Persan, vise à reconduire l’origine d’une esthétique “luxuriante et somptueuse” à la mode Levantine, Iranienne, Turque – hégémoniquement associées à la mode modeste. Elles décentrent le regard de Connie Corleone à Nedjma, ou aux figures des femmes passionnées par la liberté d'expression, la lutte au patriarcat et l'égalité des droits, et problematisent la vision de la femme fatale du Moyen-Orient, telle que construite par l'orientalisme, celle qui allie exotisme, sensualité et danger, souvent sexualisée et appropriée par l'Occident.
M: Documents d'archive ; Collections de Vetements et Accessoires ; Evenements personnelles ; Slice of life

H:
Practices : 
1. Broderie 
2. Couching
Cultures :
1. Fashion and the Digital turn
I:
We need to distinguish between the different subcategories and subthemes of the digitalisation and gamification of fashion. On the one hand, there is the digitalisation of motifs and craft practices that rely on endangered know-how, which is more or less related to fashion. Embroidery, couching motifs and woven patterns are digitised using vectorial platforms. The result of this vectorial work is then reframed by the editorial formats of the social media platforms where contents ends up. In this identified trend, digital renderings often refer to fashion practices related to diaspora and migration, helping to preserve the memory of these practices throughout migratory waves and documenting how they evolve over time and space. This is also one of the reasons behind the politicised nature of this type of content, which consistently reimagines garments that no longer exist and disseminates practices that have been dispersed around the world due to war, genocide, poverty and other adverse situations.
On the other hand, the digitalisation trend also encompasses digitally native fashion: 2D or 3D renderings of garments that do not exist in material form. These renderings are used to deconstruct the appearance of garments, rethink them, or very likely reappropriate the representation of a fashioned body that has been presented through the bias of hegemonic discourse.
J:
This type of production, which relies on pre-existing editing, synchronisation tools, and audio-visual templates provided by TikTok or circulated among users, participates in a recognisable digital genre, that of the fashion-oriented transformation, “outfit” video, motif representation and more. They conform to a codified structure that gives priority to visual iteration, rhythmic cuts, and the layering of textual and graphic elements as a mode of representation, as well as a pattern of content indexation in the way they consistently use hashtags, keywords and structures to organise and retrieve the content. As Rocamora (2012) states, the blogosphere, like Deleuze and Guattari’s (1980) rhizome, is a constantly shifting space. Its many blog pages act like conceptual surfaces (“plateaux”), opening onto an endless network of sites and signs that keep the reader in motion within an ever-evolving landscape made of hyperlinks and hypertexts, linked in a non-linear, non-hierarchical way. By applying specific and recurring elements, the creator builds a structured system that groups related videos together while also linking them to others that talk about the same topic, use the same model, or simply use the same sound track. In this way, these elements function as indexing tools, shaping how the content is archived by the platform, and discovered and interpreted by other users.
These include the integration of visuals, text, sound, and platform-native formats such as reels, carousels, and captioning. These elements work together as resources that give form to fashion heritage. This trend that digitalises, remediates or gamify fashion and related craft practices builds a recognisable, platform-native editorial identity, where the fashion object is made legible through repetition, modular structure, and digital aesthetic codes (e.g. typographic grids, 3D animation).
M: 3D et 2D ; Behind-the-scenes ; Process photographies and recordings ; Sketches

H:
Cultures :

1. Fashion and Popular Culture 
2. Fashion, Diaspora and Migration
3. Fashion and Feminism
I:
1. The Mezzogiorno and the fetishism of trauma as a commodity
The controversy surrounding this aesthetic is rooted in the "discovery" and subsequent sanitisation of a specific, violent historical context known as the "Questione Meridionale" or "The Southern Question". From an academic perspective, the "mob wife" archetype can be understood as a byproduct of the socio-economic neglect experienced by Southern Italy, which subsequently led to the rise of extra-legal governance systems such as the Camorra, Cosa Nostra and 'Ndrangheta. Critics contend that this tendency involves the fetishisation of commodities, whereby visual signifiers are detached from the labour, suffering and "bloody" history that gave rise to them.
Intracultural appropriation theory, posits that organised crime groups frequently use cultural values such as "honour" and "family" to gain legitimacy. When digital creators adopt the guise of "mafia wives" in their "cosplay", they unwittingly replicate the very codes that are employed to perpetuate systems of oppression. This aestheticisation of violence metamorphoses the moody funeral look into a romanticised performance of a grim reality characterised by domestic silence and physical abuse. For Southern Italians, these are historical traumas associated with state dysfunction, death, danger, fear. As stated by sociological perspectives on the domestic reality, the aesthetic functions as a costume made of decades of women's pain, where a dark, smoky eye represents makeup heavy enough to cover bruises and jewellery serves as an apology for the violence inherent in the lifestyle. 

The mob wife trend can be situated at a friction point between transcultural appreciation, where marginalised groups find common ground in a rejection of Anglo-centric norms of looking, and cultural erasure and appropriation. On one hand, non-Italian minorities may appropriate these codes to honour a shared immigrant experience, on the other this practice remains contentious due to its potential to reduce the profound and violent history of Southern Italy to a superficial marketing category. This process effectively divests survivors of their narrative, thereby favouring the establishment of a digital aesthetic. The transition to the mob wife is a negotiation of power and visibility within the ambit of a globalised fashion economy. 

The question that arises is how the fashion industry might adapt its practices to acknowledge an aesthetic that shares codes among immigrants (and why it does), without perpetuating the romanticisation of violence associated with organised crime.

2. The semiotics of the "immigrant aesthetic" as code remixing.
The prevailing sociological analysis suggests that the 'mob wife' look – characterised by heavy vintage furs, animal prints and bold gold jewellery – functions as a shared vernacular of the outsider. Scholars specialising in the fields of fashion and migration persuasively argue that the visual language of loudness frequently functions as a creative defence mechanism for marginalised groups. For many first- and second-generation immigrant communities extending from the Global South and Eastern Europe to the Middle East, these ostentatious markers are semiotic tools for achieving visibility.

In a Western neoliberal context, where subdued fashion is frequently associated with whiteness and high-status cultural capital, the adoption of bold, ostentatious luxury enables these communities to signal economic advancement by using loud brands to broadcast financial success in environments where they have historically been marginalised. Moreover, this style functions as a means of reclaiming space through the use of hyper-femininity and maximalism, thereby resisting the cultural invisibility imposed by the dominant fashion discourses. When non-Italian minorities engage with these codes, they are able to recognise a shared history of displacement, and perceive the mob wife as a universal symbol of the immigrant matriarch rather than a caricature. This is defined as a figure of power and strenght who navigates a hostile state through bold, unapologetic self-presentation.
J: The emergence of the "mob wife winter" or "mob wife aesthetic" on digital platforms represents a significant semiotic shift in contemporary fashion, moving away from the "quiet luxury" and "clean girl" trends toward a paradigm of maximalist ethnic performativity. The "Mob Wife" aesthetic became a popular microtrend on TikTok in early 2023 after Kayla Trivieri, a Canadian blockchain product developer living in New York City, posted a video declaring "Clean Girl is out; mob wife era is in." The video garnered over a million views and inspired many others to adopt the aesthetic. Although the aesthetic originated in 2023, it draws inspiration from earlier portrayals of Mafia wives, particularly television shows like "The Sopranos," which celebrated its 25th anniversary the same week Trivieri's video went viral. Trivieri herself acknowledged this influence, referencing the show's main character in her video. The aesthetic can be seen as a reaction against polished or "minimalistic" fashion trends. The inspiration looks at the 1970s and 1980s fashion.
M: Evenements personnels ; Slice of life ; Street fashion photography

H:
Practices : 
1. Broderie
2. Tressage
3. Macramé 
4. Weaving
5. Couture

Cultures :
1. Fashion and De-fashion
2. Sustainable Fashion
I:
The concept of degrowth stems from ambitions to balance economics with planetary boundaries. The planetary boundaries concept presents a set of 9 boundaries that we must exist within for humanity to continue to develop and thrive for generations to come. These vital thresholds, such as climate change, biodiversity and the nitrogen cycle, are already being approached or have been crossed (Milena Büchs, Max Koch,
Challenges for the degrowth transition: The debate about wellbeing, Futures, Volume 105, 2019), In fashion, degrowth starts with a rejection of the fast fashion business model. That would mean separating the production of cheap garments from profitability, producing fewer garments that we keep for longer, and repairing clothes instead of throwing them away. De-growing fashion often relies on ancient modes and practices of production, recovering low-impact techniques. But degrowth is also about stimulating and re-discovering local elements of craft and fashion heritage and communicating these creative shifts in a sustainable manner to a wide audience. It wishes as well to re-introduce traditional garments, techniques, and materials but with the consciousness of the contemporary innovative twist. This moves towards the highlighting of ancestral know-hows, rooted in the use of natural elements and low-consuption tools.
J: The fashion process tendency on social media platforms usually relies on the behind the scenes of the production of a piece (customised) or a whole collection. This might include personal engagement in the practice, or collecting of documents and inspirational materials, as well as the acknowledgement of the craft and handiwork behind the garment, made by artisans, and the cultural background of family members that perhaps shaped the practice or inspired the work. It is a sort of 'process turn' or fetishisation of the behind the scenes of production and creative work. Most common practices showcased are those concerning the parts of a garment or collection that call for a precise and often little known know-how, such as regional embroidery or weaving types (fytkiotiko, leftaritika, thrit, tatreez, chest panel, couching and embroidery, etc.) or the inspiration, research and sketching.
M: Behind-the-scenes ; Process photography and recordings ; Sketches ; Pages de livres, magazines et catalogues
N: Montages ; Cup Cuts ; Beat-sync ; Voice over

H: Fashion and Adornment of the body
I:
The performative essence of the act of fashioning the body is well represented by symbols. In visual semiotics, a symbol is an arbitrary sign and is one of three types of sign. A symbol is a sign whose relationship with its referent is arbitrary or conventional. Symbols therefore require a shared understanding or social agreement in order to be interpreted. Words, for example, are symbols: the word ‘tree’ bears no resemblance to the object ‘tree’ and is not a physical trace of it, but we use it by convention to refer to that object. In the early 20th century, Charles S. Pierce described a logic of icons by classifying signs according to the relationship they have with their object. He described the three modes of representation of a sign: the icon, the index and the symbol. The iconic sign is a figurative sign as it reproduces a real object and its characteristics with varying degrees of fidelity. The icon has a relationship of resemblance with its object; it is an analogical sign. It is easily recognisable and understandable due to the similarities it shares with its referent. The symbol has, instead, a conventional relationship with its object; it is an intellectual and arbitrary sign. 

A motif can be a unit composed of decorative elements (illustrations, graphic forms) that is repeated X times on a surface to create a visual rhythm—whether regular or irregular—thereby producing a, more or less harmonious, whole. It is a very important component of the expressive repertoire of the visual arts. It can be either a symbol or an icon, linked to its referent by resemblance or by conventions. That's why here, when talking about fashioning the body and we talk about motifs, symbols, patterns we refer as much to amazigh tattoos as to palestinian Intifada's chest panels. Fashion itself can be understood as a symbol of a certain cultural phenomenon, therefore is always communicational, it always stands for something.
